coredata.dc:title: Improved photocatalytic properties of Fe(III) ion doped Bi2MoO6 for the oxidation of organic pollutants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lu
Du, Chunyan
Tan, Shiyang
Zhang, Zhuo
Song, Jiahao
Su, Yihai
Zhang, Yin
Wang, Shitao
Yu, Guanlong
Chen, Hong
Zhou, Lu
Chen, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Improving the separation rate of electron-hole pairs by doping ion is an effective means to enhance photocatalytic activity and has aroused research interest in the development of high-efficient photocatalytic materials. To promote the degradation performance of organic pollutants like Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), an Fe3+ doped bismuth molybdate (Fe–Bi2MoO6) composite photocatalyst was synthesized using a simple hydrothermal technique. When the doping amount of Fe3+ was 1 wt%, the composite represented the highest photocatalytic activity with 92.4% RhB removal after 90 min of visible light irradiation, which was 66.3% higher than pristine Bi2MoO6 nanosheets. Trapping experiments and EPR measurements proved that the holes (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −) were the primary reactive species. Additionally, the introduction of Fe3+ broadened the light absorption range and acted as an n-type impurity to increase the electron density of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets, resulting in improved photocurrent density, increasing carrier capture and prolonging carrier lifetime. Moreover, the Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite had good stability, with its removal rate remaining at 83.2% after four cycles of reuse. The novel Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalyst was formulated with the aim of realizing a practical method to remove organic pollutants using solar light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2MoO6
Photocatalysis
Fe-doping
Visible light irradiation
RhB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photocatalytic degradation curves of the Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 for RhB under visible light irradiation; (b) first order kinetic matching plots of the corresponding photocatalysts; (c) cycling tests of the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 composite for degradation of RhB; (d) XRD patterns and (e) XPS spectra of the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 before and after four cycles.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the samples.
SEM images of (a,b) Bi2MoO6 and (c,d) Fe1–Bi2MoO6; (e) EDS scanning result of Fe1–Bi2MoO6.
XPS spectra of (a) Bi 4f, (b) Mo 3d, (c) O 1s and (d) Fe 2p.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra; (b) the band gaps of the samples; (c) Mott-Schottky plots.
(a) The PL spectra and (b) photocurrent response density of the samples.

(a) Photocatalytic performance of AGBMO-15 in the presence of trapping agents: ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) as hole scavenger, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as hydroxyl radical scavenger, benzoquinone (BQ) as superoxide radical scavenger; (b) first order kinetic matching plots of corresponding trapping agents; (c) EPR spectra of superoxide radical species trapped by DMPO in the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 sample dispersed in methanol under visible light irradiation; (d) EPR spectra of hydroxyl radical species trapped by DMPO in the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 sample dispersed in aqueous solution under vsible light irradiation.
Schematic of a possible reaction mechanism of the photocatalytic process in the presence of the Fe–Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst.
Photocatalytic degradation efficiency and kinetic rate constant (Kapp) values of RhB in the samples.

head.ce:title: Improved photocatalytic properties of Fe(III) ion doped Bi2MoO6 for the oxidation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Hydraulic Engineering, Changsha University of Science & Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Hydraulic Engineering, Changsha University of Science & Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Dongting Lake Aquatic Eco-Environmental Control and Restoration of Hunan Province, Changsha, 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Dongting Lake Aquatic Eco-Environmental Control and Restoration of Hunan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Dongting Lake Aquatic Eco-Environmental Control and Restoration of Hunan Province, Changsha, 410114, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Hydraulic Engineering, Changsha University of Science & Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving the separation rate of electron-hole pairs by doping ion is an effective means to enhance photocatalytic activity and has aroused research interest in the development of high-efficient photocatalytic materials. To promote the degradation performance of organic pollutants like Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), an Fe3+ doped bismuth molybdate (Fe–Bi2MoO6) composite photocatalyst was synthesized using a simple hydrothermal technique. When the doping amount of Fe3+ was 1 wt%, the composite represented the highest photocatalytic activity with 92.4% RhB removal after 90 min of visible light irradiation, which was 66.3% higher than pristine Bi2MoO6 nanosheets. Trapping experiments and EPR measurements proved that the holes (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −) were the primary reactive species. Additionally, the introduction of Fe3+ broadened the light absorption range and acted as an n-type impurity to increase the electron density of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets, resulting in improved photocurrent density, increasing carrier capture and prolonging carrier lifetime. Moreover, the Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite had good stability, with its removal rate remaining at 83.2% after four cycles of reuse. The novel Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalyst was formulated with the aim of realizing a practical method to remove organic pollutants using solar light.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution from organic molecules is particularly concerning [1,2]. As international environmental standards have become increasingly stringent in recent years, several approaches, including absorption [3], biodegration [4], ozonation [5] and chlorination [6], have been applied to remove organic wastes such as dyes from waste water. Photocatalysis appears to be an effective and promising technology for oxidizing and degrading organic pollutants, with the advantages of high-efficiency, photochemical-stability and cost-efficiency [7,8]. Bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6), a popular photocatalytic form of Aurivillius oxide, is especially sought-after as its superior properties making it an ideal semiconductor; these properties include a low price, corrosion resistance [9], and applicable band gap (2.50–2.80 eV) [10]. This band gap is conducive to capturing visible light and degrading organic contaminants [11]. Meanwhile, Bi2MoO6 is especially attractive by virtue of its unique layered structures, in which Bi2O2 2+ layers are sandwiched between MoO4 − slabs, promoting the formation of nanosheets or nanoplates [12,13]. However, what limits the practical application of the single Bi2MoO6 crystal is limited by its low quantum yield and high recombination rate of photogenic electron-hole pairs [14]. Improving its photocatalytic activity improves the feasibility of its use, and many methods to do so have been explored, such as surface modification [15], element doping [16], and heterogeneous construction [17]. Among these, element doping has shown a visible potential. This procedure modifies the electronic structure of Bi2MoO6, narrowingits band gap and broadening its solar radiation absorption range [18]. For example, the non-metals such as F, Cl, Br [19], and I [20], and transition metals including Ni, Ti [21], Ru [22], and Sm [23] have been successfully doped into Bi2MoO6, with the resulting materials showing obvious red shifts in their optical response wavelengths.

As a naturally abundant variable valence metal, Fe is rather stable in multiple forms [24], such as Fe3+, a common oxidant used in many industrial processes. Fe3+ is very important to the human body and widely used in everyday life [25]. Moreover, this form can capture photogenerated electrons and reduce it to Fe2+, which in turn triggers a reaction producing more hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [26]. It can also oxidize to form Fe4+, initiating a reaction to produce superoxide radicals (•O2 −). After these reactions, the resulting Fe2+ and Fe4+ can then be recovered in its Fe3+ form, allowing Fe2+ to be reused without deactivation [27]. This in turn can easily be doped into the Bi2MoO6 crystal lattice because it is of a suitable size [28], and overall is an excellent candidate for Bi2MoO6 doping.

Meng et al. [29] used Fe-mediation to obtain an efficient Bi2MoO6 nitrogen-fixing photocatalyst, which exhibits a decrease in the surface work function due to the doped Fe, boosting the charge transport to the surface of the catalyst. It can also improve the charge collection through a Fe3+/Fe2+ redox pathway, serving as an active site for nitrogen reduction. Moreover, Fe-mediated Bi2MoO6 exhibits dramatically enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity for nitrogen fixation, in sharp contrast with pristine Bi2MoO6.

To verify the above theoretical analysis, we prepared an Fe3+ doped Bi2MoO6 (Fe–Bi2MoO6) composite with improved photocatalytic properties through a simple hydrothermal technique and optimized its Fe3+content. The degradation efficiency of this novel photocatalyst under visible light was investigated with Rhodamine B (RhB) as the target pollutant. After 90 min of activity tests, it was found that when the content of Fe3+ was 1 wt%, the photocatalytic performance reached the highest removal efficiency of 92.4%. In addition, the degradation rate remained at 83.2% after four repeated experiments. Furthermore, after the introduction of Fe3+, the compound's spectral range was red shifted and its separation efficiency was also improved. Therefore, it is a potential photocatalytic material for further practical application in wastewater treatment with organic pollutants.

The Bi2MoO6 was prepared via a traditional solvothermal method [30–32]. In detail, bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 98.0%) and ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 99.0%) with a Bi/Mo mole ratio of 2.0 were dissolved in 20 mL nitric acid (HNO3, 15 mol/L) and deionized water, respectively. After 30 min of stirring, the mixed (NH4)6Mo7O24 solution was cautiously added to the mixed Bi(NO3)3 solution. Then 1 wt% ferric nitrate pentahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.0%) was dispersed in the above mixed solution and stirred continuously for 20 min. Ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 10 mol/L) was used to adjust the pH of the resultant mixture to a value of 9. Afterwards, the suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated up to 160 °C for 16 h. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, it was filtered and washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. Lastly, the collected sediments were dried at 60 °C for 24 h and labeled as Fe1–Bi2MoO6. Analogously, Fe0.5-Bi2MoO6, Fe0.8-Bi2MoO6, Fe1.2-Bi2MoO6 and Fe1.5-Bi2MoO6 samples were also synthesized.

The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were characterized via a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Tescan Mira3 microscope, the energy spectrum model is Oxford XMAX20). The crystalline phases and structures of the prepared samples were investigated by Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance Ultima IV diffractometer). The elemental and chemical state of the surface of the sample was determined by Versa Probe PHI 5000 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, USA ThermoFischer, ESCALABXi+, U = 12.5 kV, I = 16 mA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded with a PerkinElmer Spectrum GX FT-IR spectrometer (scanning range: 4000–500 cm−1). UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained with a PE lambda 750S UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the 350 nm excitation source at room temperature. Photoelectric properties were performed by the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China) which an Ag/AgCl (0.2 M Na2SO4) as the reference electrode and a Pt plate as the counter electrode. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker A 300, Germany) measurements were carried out to identify •O2 − and •OH with the 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trapping agent.

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) was investigated by degrading RhB at room temperature. A xenon lamp (300 W) was utilized as the illuminant and a cutoff filter was used to occlude light with a wavelength lower than 420 nm. Typically, 0.5 g/L of the as-prepared sample was weighed and then dissolved in 100 mL of RhB (10 mg/L) solution. The subsequent photodegradation process was facilitated by the photocatalytic adsorption of the pollutant. To bring the reaction solution to adsorption desorption-equilibrium before photodegradation commenced, the reaction solution was stirred in the dark for 20 min before the photodegradation process. Afterwards, the magnetically stirred suspension was placed under the xenon lamp, with the distance between the aqueous surface and the light source at approximately 10 cm. During photocatalytic degradation, a 3 mL portion of aliquot from the solution was sampled at 10 min intervals. A UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the solution in the transparent upper layer. The photodegradation efficiency of RhB can be calculated according to Eq. (1) as follows [33]:
(1) Degradation Efficiency (%) = (1− C t / C 0) × 100%
where C 0 represents the initial concentration of RhB, and C t expresses the concentration of RhB at time t. The band gap energy of the semiconductor can be calculated by the Kubelka-Munk function based on the UV–vis DRS spectrum [34], as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
αhν = A(hν−Eg) n/2
where α, h, v, Eg, and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, and band gap and constant, respectively.

The effectiveness of the photocatalytic RhB degradation (expressed as standard concentration C/C0) of the doped Bi2MoO6 composite with different mass fractions of Fe3+ doping is shown in Fig. 1 a. After 90 min of illumination, the RhB removal rate of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 peaked at 92.4%, which was 66.3% higher than that of pure Bi2MoO6. When the Fe3+ content was less than or equal to 1 wt%, the degradation rate of RhB increased with the increase of Fe3+. However, the photocatalytic activity decreased when Fe3+ doping amounts exceeded 1 wt%. This might be due to the fact that a moderate amount of Fe3+ leads to more carriers being captured, improving the photocatalytic performance [35]. When the doping amount of Fe3+ exceeded a certain threshold, carrier recombination centers formed and the photogenerated carriers tended to fall into the impure sites and lose their activity. Besides, excessive doping caused aggregation and reduced the effective surface, hindering the absorption of visible light [36]. To further explore the photocatalytic activity, the first-kinetic fitting of the experiment was carried out based on the Langmuir Hinshelwood (L-H) model [37], as shown in Fig. 1b. The order of the K value was Fe1–Bi2MoO6 (0.02305 min−1) > Fe1.2-Bi2MoO6 (0.01836 min−1) > Fe0.8-Bi2MoO6 (0.01665 min−1) > Fe1.5-Bi2MoO6 (0.01306 min−1) > Fe0.5-Bi2MoO6 (0.01038 min−1) > Bi2MoO6 (0.00032 min−1). The corresponding degradation rates and K values of acquired samples are summarized in Table 1 .

Stability and reusability are essential characteristics of photocatalysts [38]. Therefore, to further test the stability of the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 sample, four cycles of photocatalytic degradation of RhB were carried out. At the end of each cycle, the composite photocatalyst was separated from the suspension by suction filtration and washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. These collected photocatalyst was dried at 60 °C overnight, then re-dispersed in the new RhB solution under the same reaction conditions as the previous cycles. As shown in Fig. 1c, after four repeated cycles, the removal rate of RhB dye by Fe1–Bi2MoO6 decreased to 83.2%. Additionally, the stability of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 before and after continuous cycles was analyzed by XRD and XPS before and after the degradation cycles, the results of which are shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. 1e. Both the XRD pattern and XPS spectra of the sample after four cycles did not change significantly compared to the fresh composite, indicating that the structure and crystalline phase of the used Fe1–Bi2MoO6 remained unchanged. This further confirmed the favorable reusability and stability of the composite.

To determine if the Fe3+ doping procedure was successful, the as-prepared Fe–Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts with different Fe3+ contents were analyzed using XRD. In Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of pure Bi2MoO6 at 2θ = 28.3°, 32.9°, 35.9°, 47.1°, 55.9°, and 58.9° could be attributed to (131), (200), (151), (062), (331), and (262) crystal planes of γ-Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 76–2388) [39], respectively. All the XRD patterns of the Fe–Bi2MoO6 samples had similar diffraction peak positions to the original Bi2MoO6 spectra, but no other phase separation associated with Fe metal or its related oxides was found, indicating that the introduction of Fe3+ resulted in doped Fe–Bi2MoO6 rather than BiFeO3 [29]. Additionally, due to the low concentration of Fe3+, it would be probably hard to detect ultrafine particles forming a kind of clusters with sizes in the nanometer range. Furthermore, there was a shift of (200) and (331) peaks in Fe–Bi2MoO6 composites, which further confirmed successful doping with Fe3+. Moreover, we can analyze Fe3+ doping according to Bragg's Law (Eq (3)) [40]:
(3) d(hkl) = nλ/(2sinθ)

Where d represents the distance between the parallel atomic planes, λ expresses the incident wave length, and θ is the angle between the incident light and the crystal plane. The ionic radii of O2−, Mo6+, Bi3+, and Fe3+ are 0.140 nm,0.062 nm, 0.103 nm, and 0.055 nm [29], respectively, and the angle constant of the sample increased in the direction of the low angle diffraction. Therefore, Fe3+ could not replace other ions, and it was likely doped in the lattice gap, resulting in the increase of crystal plane spacing.

The surface chemical bonds of synthesized samples were investigated using FT-IR. Fig. 2b shows that when Bi2MoO6 was doped with Fe3+, there was no new peak. The characteristic absorption peaks of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 matched well with pristine Bi2MoO6, indicating that no other impurities were introduced. Among them, the absorption peaks between 500 cm−1 to 600 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 to 950 cm−1 could be attributed to the vibration peaks of Bi–O modes and Mo–O modes, respectively [41]. Furthermore, the absorption band at 690 cm−1 was caused by the bending vibration and asymmetric stretching vibration mode of MoO6 [42]. The strength of the absorption peak of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 was weakened, implying that Fe3+ doping could reduce the asymmetric stretching vibration of MoO6. Additionally, the peak positions of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 samples were shifted slightly, indicating that the doping with Fe3+ affected the Bi–O modes and Mo–O modes. This could be attributed to the doping of Fe3+ into the Bi2O2 2+ interlayer [30].

SEM was used to examine the surface morphology of the doped compound. In Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3c, the pure Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 samples synthesized using the solvothermal procedure were characterized by irregular nanoplate morphology with a smooth appearance. Fig. 3b shows that the thickness of the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets was in the range of 100–145 nm. To further investigate whether the Fe3+ doping was successful, EDS point scanning was carried out on the area shown in Fig. 3d. The results (Fig. 3e) imply that the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 composite contained Bi, Mo, O, and Fe elements.

To analyze the state of surface elements and chemical compositions, high-resolution XPS spectra at different binding energies were measured. Fig. 4 a showed that Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of pure Bi2MoO6 had two peaks at 159.5 eV and 164.8 eV, indicating that Bi was present in its Bi3+ form. As shown in Fig. 4b, the peaks of binding energy in Bi2MoO6 at 232.8 eV and 236.0 eV could be attributed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, implying the existence of Mo6+ [43]. In Fig. 4c, three peaks located at binding energies of 530.2 eV, 530.6 eV, and 531.4 eV in the O 1s spectrum of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 might be designated as Bi–O, Mo–O, and the oxygen in the surface bound hydroxyl (–OH) [44]. The peak positions of Bi 4f and O 1s in the XPS spectrum of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 shifted to lower values compared with pristine Bi2MoO6, owing to the electronegativity of Fe3+, which is higher than that of Bi3+ [45]. With regard to Mo 3d, this phenomenon might be due to the migration of electrons from Fe3+ to Mo 3d [46]. Two weak peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 were observed in Fig. 4d, which were located at 711.9 eV and 724.9 eV respectively, indicating the presence of Fe3+. Additionally, the difference between the two peaks was 13.0 eV [44]; further evidence indicate it to have been trivalent Fe3+. Therefore, it could be concluded that Fe3+ was successfully doped into the Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the samples. The strong absorption peak of pure Bi2MoO6 was at about 350 nm, and the absorption edge was at about 476 nm. When Fe3+ was introduced, light absorption wavelength of the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 sample shifted to 536 nm. The plot of (αhν)2 against photo energy (hν) can be obtained with the aid of the Kubelka-Munk formula [23]. From the intersection point of tangent lines on the coordinate axis in Fig. 5b, the band gap edges (Eg) of the pure Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst were observed to be about 2.70 eV and 2.51 eV, respectively.

The Mott-Schottky technique was used to analyze the location of new isolated impurity levels and measured by the electrochemical station. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, obviously, the flat-band potentials (E
FB ) of Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 were −0.29 eV and −0.39 eV, respectively. The slopes of the Mott-Schottky curves for Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 were both positive, indicating that the two photocatalysts were n-type semiconductors [47]. As the conduction band (E
CB ) of n-type semiconductors is relatively close to that of its E
FB and deemed to be more negative about 0.1 eV than its E
FB [48]. Thus, the E
CB values of Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 were −0.39 eV and −0.49 eV, respectively.

The potentials of semiconductors valence band (E
VB ) can be theoretically calculated according to the electronegativity principle, generally determined by the empirical formula in Eq. (4) [17]:
(4)
E
VB
= E
CB
+ Eg

Using this formula, the E
VB values of Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 were calculated to be 2.31 and 2.02 eV, respectively. It was possible that a new energy level composed of Fe 2p and O 2p was formed at the top of the VB of Fe-doped Bi2MoO6 [29], which reduced the band gap and resulted in easier transition of the photogenerated carriers.

PL spectroscopy was used to investigate the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. As shown in Fig. 6a, when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm, an obvious emission peak of pure Bi2MoO6 and Fe1–Bi2MoO6 was detected near 470 nm, which could be used to reflect the recombination of photogenerated carriers. The intensity of Fe1–Bi2MoO6 appeared to be weaker than that of the original Bi2MoO6, suggesting that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was reduced after Fe3+ doping [39]. This was mainly due to the fact that Fe3+, as a carrier capture center, promoted the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improved the photocatalytic activity.

To further understand the migration and separation of photogenerated carriers in the Fe1–Bi2MoO6 sample, the photocurrent response analysis was carried out with the result shown in Fig. 6b. In four light and dark cycle experiments, it was found that the photocurrent density of Fe3+ doped photocatalyst was significantly higher than that of pristine Bi2MoO6. This phenomenon further demonstrated that the introduction of Fe3+ improved the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

To verify the major active species in the degradation process, a free radical trapping agent experiment was carried out. Fig. 7 a shows the performance curve of RhB degradation by Fe1–Bi2MoO6 with different scavengers. Explicitly, when 1 mM isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was added, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB declined slightly, but this had little effect on the overall degradation experiment. Therefore, •OH was not the main active species. On the contrary, the removal efficiency of RhB decreased to 21.4% and 44.7% respectively after introducing ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and benzoquinone (BQ). Thus, it was deduced that holes (h+) and •O2 − were the dominant active species. The first-kinetic fitting parameters from the experiment are shown in Fig. 7b. The order of K value was IPA (0.01563 min−1) > BQ (0.00399 min−1) > EDTA-2Na (0.00157 min−1).

To further corroborate the formation of •O2 − and •OH in the degradation process, the •O2 − in methanol and •OH in aqueous solution were fist captured using DMPO as trapping reagent; then the EPR was used to detect the DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH in the solution. The test was carried out under dark and visible light, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7c, the characteristic peaks of •O2 − with the obvious intensity were captured by DMPO under visible light, implying that when the system was excited by light, large amounts of •O2 − is produced. However, Fig. 7d shows that •OH was not detected in the dark or under visible light, indicating that •OH was not generated during the degradation process. Based on the above research, h+ and •O2 − acted as the major species responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic oxidation organic reactions.

Based on above photocatalytic degradation performance and trapping experiments, a mechanism for doping photocatalytic Bi2MoO6 with Fe3+ was proposed. Fe3+ doped Bi2MoO6, reduced the band gap and enhanced the absorption of visible light. Additionally, the Fe3+ could act as an n-type impurity to increase the electron density of Bi2MoO6 [29], thus improving its photocurrent density under visible light, increasing carrier capture, prolonging carrier lifetime, and ultimately enhancing photocatalytic activity. In Fig. 8 , it can be seen that when the Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite was irradiated by sunlight with energy equal to or greater than the band gap energy, the electrons were transferred from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) (route I, Eq (6)). Furthermore, electrons could be photo-excited from the CB of Fe–Bi2MoO6 to the energy level of Fe3+/Fe2+ as well (route II) [49]. Therefore, more photons from the visible light source can be exploited to form photo-generated electrons and holes. In the scavenging experiments, h+ acting as the key active species directly oxidized RhB dye and degraded it into CO2, H2O and other small molecular products (Eq (8)). Moreover, the E
CB (−0.49 eV) of Fe–Bi2MoO6 was more negative than the reduction potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV) [50], so O2 absorbed on the surface of Fe–Bi2MoO6 was reduced to •O2 − by electrons (Eq (7)). Thus, the separation of the photo-generated electrons and holes was promoted, further improving the compound's properties. •O2 − has strong oxidizability and can involve in degradation of RhB (Eq (9)). However, the standard redox potential of H2O/•OH (2.38 eV) was higher than the E
VB of Fe–Bi2MoO6 (2.02 eV) [51]. Therefore, the photogenerated holes on the VB of Fe–Bi2MoO6 could not oxidize OH− to generate •OH species. The electron-hole transfer path could be determined by the following formulas [32]:

Generation of photoelectron-hole pairs:
(6) Fe–Bi2MoO6 + hν → h+(Fe–Bi2MoO6) + e–(Fe–Bi2MoO6)

Migration and transformation of photoelectron-hole pairs:
(7) e–(Fe–Bi2MoO6) + O2 → Fe–Bi2MoO6 + •O2 −

Degradation of pollutants:
(8) RhB + h+ → Degradation product + CO2 + H2O (major reaction)
(9) RhB + •O2 − → Degradation product + CO2 + H2O (secondary reaction)

An Fe–Bi2MoO6 composite with improved photocatalytic properties was successfully synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method, and the XRD results showed that Fe3+ was successfully incorporated into the lattice of Bi2MoO6. The Fe1–Bi2MoO6 composite material displayed the highest photocatalytic performance in removing 92.4% of RhB within 90 min of visible light irradiation, which was 66.8% higher than that of pure Bi2MoO6. This outstanding performance could be attributed to the red shift in the absorption spectrum range and a reduced band gap. The free radical scavenger experiment and EPR measurements revealed that h+ and •O2 − to be the major active species. Therefore, both of h+ and •O2 − were involved in the degradation of RhB. Conclusively, the synthesized Fe–Bi2MoO6 might be considered to be a highly efficient photocatalyst for the future treatment of contaminated water under solar light irradiation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Hunan Provincial Department of Education Science and Technology Project (18B127, 19A032), the National Natural Science Foundation of China of China (51109016, 51308068, 51308069, 51809019), the National Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2020JJ4602).
